Energy Flows
Total load split into Grid Offtake and Solar Autoconsumption (Grid Offtake + Solar Autoconsumption = Total Load)
Spot Exposition
Consumption split between solar-hedged volume and spot-price-exposed volume
Hedging logic (Setup 1): Solar autoconsumption acts as a natural hedge. The remaining load (Grid Offtake) is fully exposed to day-ahead spot prices.
Flexible Load
Load served by battery discharge — shifted from solar excess to later consumption
Setup 1 has no battery — flexible load is zero. Switch to Future to see BESS load shifting.

vs Other Scenarios
Offtake Cost — A · Retail
Offtake cost at Day-Ahead spot price + €40/MWh retail surcharge
A · Retail: Grid Offtake × (Day-Ahead spot price + €40/MWh surcharge). Cost floats with the spot market — no advance commitment required.
Hedging & Spot Exposition — Volumes
Consumption split between hedged and spot-exposed volume (MWh)
Hedged volume: Solar autoconsumption — on-site generation that avoids any grid purchase.  ·  Spot-exposed volume: Grid offtake priced at DA spot + €40/MWh surcharge.
Click Strategy — C · Optimised
Clicked vs spot-exposed volume with hedge ratio overlay (right axis)
Strategy: Months ranked by historical average DA spot price. Cheapest months → 20% hedged (benefit from low spot). Most expensive months → 80% hedged (cap costs). All intermediate months scaled linearly between 20% and 80%.  ·  Assumption: future price seasonality mirrors historical patterns.
Offtake Response
Load shifting attractiveness by hour of day
View
Date
Monthly Average — Attractiveness by Hour
Average score per hour-of-day across the selected month
Score:
1 · Avoid
2 · Unattractive
3 · Neutral
4 · Attractive
5 · Ideal
How the Score Works
Understanding the load shifting attractiveness score
Each hour receives a score from 1 · Avoid to 5 · Ideal indicating how favorable it is to shift or increase electricity consumption in that hour.

DA Spot Price: hours with low market prices are more cost-effective for additional offtake.   Solar Availability: high on-site solar production means added consumption may be covered by free generation rather than grid.   Grid Headroom: hours where existing grid offtake is low indicate the site has more spare capacity for flexible load.   BESS Availability: when the battery is not actively discharging, it has spare capacity to support additional load — making that hour more attractive for flexible consumption. This signal is active in Setup 2 (with BESS).

In Day view, scores are shown for each hour of the selected date. In Month view, each bar shows the average score for that hour-of-day across all days in the selected month — revealing recurring daily patterns regardless of day-to-day variation. All scores are normalized year-wide so they remain comparable across periods.

BESS Piloting
Battery charge / discharge & state of charge · 1 MW / 2.5 MWh
Solid bars/line = realized dispatch  ·  Transparent bars / dashed line = planned dispatch
Load
Site consumption · 15-min resolution · MW
Bars = measured values (past)  ·  Dotted line = D-1 forecast (full day)
Solar Production
On-site solar generation · 15-min resolution · MW
Bars = measured values (past)  ·  Dotted line = D-1 forecast (full day)
Prices
Day-ahead spot & imbalance prices · 15-min resolution · €/MWh
DA Spot = fully known for the day (day-ahead auction)  ·  Imbalance solid = realized  ·  Imbalance dashed = not yet known
Power Forward Prices — 2025
Monthly average clicked (quarterly fixed) vs DA spot · €/MWh · full year view
Clicked (B · quarterly fixed): the locked-in €/MWh rate for each quarter — constant within Q1 / Q2 / Q3 / Q4, updated when the client clicks a new quarter.   DA Spot (monthly avg): realized average spot price per month.   Retail (spot + €40): what the site would pay with no fixed contract. When the clicked bar sits below the retail line, the quarterly click was financially beneficial.
